ethical_notes: ETHICS: Kerma is one of Africa's earliest urban civilizations and Egypt's first serious rival to the south. Egyptian sources called it 'Kush' and depicted Kermans as enemies in royal propaganda — a perspective that long dominated historiography. Kerma's own record is archaeological, including the massive Western Deffufa mud-brick temple and elite tumulus burials with retainer sacrifices. Western scholarship (Reisner, early 20th c.) initially attributed Kerma's achievements to hypothetical 'Egyptian colonists', a racist framework refuted by Bonnet's excavations from 1977. The kingdom was destroyed by Thutmose I of Egypt c.1500 BCE, initiating centuries of Egyptian occupation of Nubia. Separate entity from later Napatan/Meroitic Kush because the material culture, capital, and political system were distinct. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-2000, strategy=exact_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.source: Bonnet, C. The Nubian Pharaohs (2006)

territory_changes.description: Thutmose I of Egypt's 18th Dynasty campaigned up the Nile, sacking Kerma. The city was burned, the ruling elite executed or deported, and Egyptian occupation of Nubia began that would last ~400 years.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Nobadia was the northernmost of the three Christian Nubian kingdoms (with Makuria and Alodia). Founded by Noba/Nobatae peoples who moved into Lower Nubia after the fall of Meroe. Converted to Miaphysite Christianity around 543 CE through missions sent by Empress Theodora, deliberately bypassing Justinian's Chalcedonian embassy — a striking case of a small African kingdom navigating Byzantine doctrinal politics for its own religious alignment. Absorbed into Makuria c.707 CE. Western scholarship long treated Christian Nubia as marginal; the Polish excavations at Faras (rescued before the Aswan Dam flooded the site) revolutionized the field by revealing sophisticated frescos, literate Coptic/Old Nubian bureaucracy, and a durable Christian culture that lasted nearly a millennium. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (500, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

territory_changes.description: King Silko's successor formally adopted Miaphysite Christianity via missions sponsored by Empress Theodora. The Miaphysite choice allied Nubia with Coptic Egypt rather than Constantinople, shaping the kingdom's theological identity for centuries.

Arab Muslim forces under Abdallah ibn Sa'd invaded Nubia; after stiff resistance (Nobadian archers were particularly feared, described in Arab sources as 'pupil-smiters'), the Baqt treaty was negotiated — an unusual long-lasting Muslim-Christian agreement involving annual exchange of slaves for grain/cloth, not tribute of submission.
Nobadia was absorbed into the Kingdom of Makuria under King Merkurios ('the new Constantine'), creating a unified Christian Nubian state from the First to the Fifth Cataract.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Takedda (identified with the Azelik archaeological site in modern Niger) was a major Saharan copper-mining and trade town described in detail by Ibn Battuta in 1353. Copper ingots from Takedda circulated widely as currency in West Africa. The Tuareg/Berber population worked the mines using enslaved labor — a rare medieval Saharan case documented by Ibn Battuta firsthand. The town was tributary to Mali and later Songhai but retained substantial autonomy. Sources are almost entirely external (Arab geographers); the indigenous perspective is archaeological only. Decline after 1500 linked to shifting trans-Saharan trade routes.

name_variants.name: Takadda
name_variants.source: Ibn Battuta, Rihla (trans. Gibb 1962)

name_variants.name: Azelik
name_variants.context: modern Tuareg name of the archaeological site identified with medieval Takedda
name_variants.source: Bernus, S. and Gouletquer, P. in Journal des Africanistes (1976)

name_variants.name: Tademekka (distinct)
name_variants.context: NOTE: Tademekka is a separate but sometimes confused site further west; not the same as Takedda
name_variants.source: Insoll, T. The Archaeology of Islam in Sub-Saharan Africa (2003)

territory_changes.description: Ibn Battuta visited Takedda on his return from Mali, staying with Berber copper merchants. His account describes mine shafts worked by enslaved laborers, red and yellow copper ingots used as currency, and local political autonomy under the Sultan of Takedda while still acknowledging Mali suzerainty.

Decline attributed to competition from alternative trans-Saharan routes, Songhai overreach, and the Moroccan invasion of Songhai in 1591 which disrupted regional trade networks. Exact date of abandonment is unclear.

sources.citation: Ibn Battuta. The Travels of Ibn Battuta, A.D. 1325-1354. Trans. H.A.R. Gibb. Cambridge: Hakluyt Society, 1962. Vol. 4 contains the Takedda material.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: One of the seven original Hausa Bakwai states. The existing 'Hausa Bakwai' entity in the DB is a collective — Katsina, Kano, Zazzau (Zaria), Gobir, Daura, Rano, and Biram were each sovereign city-states with distinct dynasties, rivalries, and trajectories. Creating them individually matches the historical reality: Kano and Katsina were the great trade rivals, Gobir the northern frontier against the Tuareg, Zazzau a southern slave-raiding state. The Habe dynasties were overthrown by the Sokoto jihad of Usman dan Fodio (1804-1808). Katsina specifically was a major Islamic scholarly center — the 16th c. Timbuktu-trained jurist Muhammad ibn al-Sabbagh al-Katsinawi worked here. ETHICS: Katsina participated in the trans-Saharan slave trade, exporting captives northward via Agadez.

name_variants.source: Usman, Y.B. The Transformation of Katsina 1400-1883 (1981)

name_variants.name: Birnin Katsina

territory_changes.description: Katsina emerged as a primary terminus of the trans-Saharan trade with Tripoli and Ghadames, surpassing Kano in commercial importance during the 15th-16th centuries. Wangara (Mande) merchants introduced Islam and literacy from the west.

Fulani jihadists under the flag-bearer Umaru Dallaji, following Usman dan Fodio, overthrew Sarki Magajin Halidu. Katsina was absorbed into the Sokoto Caliphate as an emirate under Fulani rule; the Habe dynasty fled and established Maradi in what is now Niger, continuing the Katsina line in exile.

sources.citation: Usman, Yusufu Bala. The Transformation of Katsina, 1400-1883. Zaria: Ahmadu Bello University Press, 1981
Smith, Michael G. Government in Zazzau, 1800-1950. London: Oxford University Press, 1960
Palmer, H.R. The Kano Chronicle. Journal of the Royal Anthropological Institute 38 (1908): 58-98

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Zazzau (the kingdom; Zaria the capital city, named after a 16th c. princess) was the southernmost of the Hausa Bakwai states. Famous for the warrior-queen Amina (c.1533-1610), who expanded Zazzau's territory through conquest south to the Niger-Benue confluence. ETHICS — uncomfortable truth: Zazzau's wealth derived heavily from slave-raiding on non-Muslim populations to the south (Nupe, pagan minorities), supplying both domestic slave economy and trans-Saharan export. This is essential context: Amina is celebrated today as a feminist icon but her wars produced massive enslavement. The Habe dynasty was overthrown in 1808 by Fulani jihad under Mallam Musa; the Zazzagawa royal family fled south to found Abuja (original — not the modern federal capital).

name_variants.name: Zaria Emirate
name_variants.source: Smith, M.G. Government in Zazzau (1960)

name_variants.name: Zazzau
name_variants.source: Smith, M.G. The Affairs of Daura (1978)

name_variants.name: Zegzeg
name_variants.context: Arabic rendering in trans-Saharan trade sources
name_variants.source: Hunwick, J. Arabic Literature of Africa (1995)

territory_changes.description: Queen Amina's reign (traditional dating varies, c.1576-1610) expanded Zazzau through military campaigns as far south as Nupe and Jukun lands, establishing tributary relations and capturing vast numbers of slaves. Zaria ramparts ('ganuwar Amina') are attributed to her defense building.

Fulani jihadist Mallam Musa conquered Zaria city, deposing Sarki Makau. The Habe royal family fled south and founded a new capital at Zuba (later Abuja). Zazzau became an emirate of the Sokoto Caliphate.

sources.citation: Smith, Michael G. Government in Zazzau, 1800-1950. London: Oxford University Press, 1960
Last, Murray. The Sokoto Caliphate. London: Longmans, 1967

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Gobir was the northernmost Hausa Bakwai state, a frontier polity that bore the brunt of Tuareg raids from the north and served as the ideological birthplace of the Sokoto jihad. Shehu Usman dan Fodio was a Fulani scholar in Gobir who preached against Hausa rulers' perceived syncretism with traditional religion and their enslavement of Muslims. When Sultan Yunfa turned on him in 1804, Dan Fodio's hijra to Gudu and subsequent jihad destroyed the Habe dynasty. ETHICS: the jihad narrative is contested — some historians (Last, Hiskett) emphasize religious reform; others (Clapperton's era observers, and revisionists) emphasize Fulani ethnic consolidation and the fact that the jihadists themselves extensively enslaved defeated populations including Muslims they classified as insufficient in faith.

name_variants.name: Gobir
name_variants.source: Last, M. The Sokoto Caliphate (1967)

territory_changes.description: After Sultan Yunfa of Gobir attempted to arrest the Fulani scholar Usman dan Fodio, Dan Fodio led his followers in hijra to Gudu (21 February 1804) and declared jihad. The Battle of Tabkin Kwotto (June 1804) saw the jihadists defeat a Gobir army despite being outnumbered.

Jihadist forces under Muhammad Bello captured Alkalawa. Sultan Yunfa was killed. The Gobir royal line continued in exile at Tsibiri and later moved further north to what became French territory, surviving into the 20th century as a shadow court.

sources.citation: Last, Murray. The Sokoto Caliphate. London: Longmans, 1967
Hiskett, Mervyn. The Sword of Truth: The Life and Times of the Shehu Usuman dan Fodio. New York: Oxford University Press, 1973

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Daura holds a special status in Hausa oral tradition as the 'mother' of the Bakwai — the legend of Bayajidda killing the sacred snake Sarki in the well at Daura and marrying Queen Daurama is considered by many Hausa as the founding myth of all seven legitimate Hausa states. This origin narrative is transmitted in the Bayajidda legend (variant texts in Arabic and Hausa). Modern historiography treats the Bayajidda story as ideological rather than literally historical, but it remains central to Hausa political identity. Daura itself was politically smaller than Kano or Katsina but carried ritual seniority. Overthrown 1805 in the Sokoto jihad; Habe royal line retreated to Zinder (now Niger) and Maradi areas.

name_variants.name: Daura
name_variants.source: Smith, M.G. The Affairs of Daura (1978)

territory_changes.description: Fulani jihadist Mallam Ishiaku conquered Daura for the Sokoto Caliphate. The Habe dynasty fled to Zango and eventually to Maradi and Zinder, where they continued to rule in exile.

sources.citation: Smith, Michael G. The Affairs of Daura: History and Change in a Hausa State 1800-1958. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1978
Palmer, H.R. Sudanese Memoirs Vol. III (Bayajidda legend). Lagos: Government Printer, 1928

ethical_notes: ETHICS: 'Wangara' in Arabic sources designates the gold-producing region and the Mande-speaking (Soninke/Malinke/Dyula) merchant network that operated the trans-Saharan gold trade. Wangara is not a unified state — it's a merchant diaspora and source-area that shaped political formations from Ghana to Mali to the Hausa states. Arab geographers (al-Bakri, al-Idrisi, Ibn Battuta) wrote extensively about 'Wangara gold' without understanding its source. Indigenous mining communities deliberately maintained secrecy about mine locations (Bure in modern Guinea, Bambuk on the Senegal-Faleme). Confidence is moderate because 'Wangara' was simultaneously a region, an ethnonym, and a network rather than a bounded polity — any entity record is an approximation. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1100, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

territory_changes.description: Mansa Musa's hajj caravan demonstrated the scale of Wangara gold output. Al-Umari, writing in Cairo, recorded that the king of Mali received 6000 mithqals of gold annually in tribute from the Wangara mines.

Atlantic maritime trade (Portuguese on the coast) progressively shifted gold exports southward away from trans-Saharan Wangara networks, contributing to the decline of the classical Wangara commercial system, though Dyula merchant networks persisted.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Sosso kingdom under Sumanguru Kante (Soumaoro in Mande epic) briefly filled the vacuum left by Wagadou's decline, dominating the Middle Niger area c.1200-1235. Sumanguru is the villain of the Sundiata epic — the sorcerer-king defeated at the Battle of Kirina (1235) by Sundiata Keita, which founded Mali. Sources are almost entirely the Mande oral epic tradition (jeli/griot) and later Arabic accounts derived from it; archaeology is very thin. Western historiography long either ignored Sosso or treated the Sundiata epic as fable. Current scholarship (Conrad, Jansen) treats the epic as structured historical memory with a genuine political core. The Sosso were Soninke (same people as Wagadou) but of the Kante clan, traditionally smiths and sorcerers. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1200, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Sosso Kingdom
name_variants.source: Conrad, D.C. Sunjata: A West African Epic (2004)

name_variants.name: Soso
name_variants.context: Soninke spelling of the kingdom's name
name_variants.source: Niane, D.T. Soundjata (1960)

name_variants.name: Susu Empire
name_variants.context: alternative English rendering (not to be confused with the modern Susu people of Guinea)

territory_changes.description: Sumanguru Kante captured Koumbi Saleh, completing the absorption of the crumbling Ghana Empire. This made Sosso the hegemon of the Western Sahel.

Battle of Kirina: Sundiata Keita's Mandinka coalition defeated Sumanguru. The epic tradition describes Sundiata using a white cock's spur arrow (Sumanguru's magical weakness) to defeat the sorcerer-king. The Sosso state collapsed and its territories became the core of the Mali Empire.

sources.citation: Conrad, David C. Sunjata: A West African Epic of the Mande Peoples. Indianapolis: Hackett, 2004

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Ile-Ife (classical period 1100-1500 CE) was the religious and cultural cradle of the Yoruba world and produced some of the finest naturalistic bronze and terracotta sculpture in pre-modern world art. The Ife heads, rediscovered by Leo Frobenius in 1910, were so sophisticated that Frobenius proposed they were remnants of Atlantis — a racist conjecture refuted by later archaeology (Frank Willett, Ekpo Eyo) which firmly established indigenous Yoruba origin. ETHICS: British colonial looting and illegal art-market traffic have scattered Ife heads across Western museums; restitution remains an active dispute. The existing DB entity 'Oyo' covers the later Yoruba imperial successor state — Ife is the earlier, primary religious center distinct from Oyo. Ife's decline coincided with Oyo and Benin's political rise. Present-day Ife is still the ritual center of Yoruba traditional religion, and the Ooni of Ife retains ceremonial seniority over Yoruba obaship. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1100, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Ile-Ife
name_variants.source: Willett, F. Ife in the History of West African Sculpture (1967)

name_variants.name: Ilé-Ifẹ̀
name_variants.source: Akinjogbin, I.A. (ed.) The Cradle of a Race: Ife from the Beginning to 1980 (1992)

territory_changes.description: Start of the 'classical period' of Ife art, producing the naturalistic bronze and terracotta heads. Ile-Ife became the paramount ritual center from which other Yoruba dynastic lines claimed descent via Oduduwa.
Political and artistic decline of Ile-Ife's classical period, coinciding with the rise of Oyo and Benin. The ritual role of the Ooni was preserved, but secular power shifted decisively away from Ife.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: This entity covers the EARLY pre-imperial phase of Kaabu (c.1200-1500), while the existing DB entity 'Kaabu' covers the later imperial phase (1537-1867). The early Kaabu was a frontier province of the Mali Empire, established when Sundiata's general Tiramakhan Traore conquered the Senegambia region. During this period Kaabu was a group of Mandinka chiefdoms (the 'Nsiiw' — tigers, referring to the warrior-aristocracy) tributary to Mali. Only after Mali's decline (late 16th c.) did Kaabu become an independent empire. Pre-1500 sources are almost entirely Mande oral tradition (Guelowar and Nyancho epic cycles). Confidence is low because dates are highly approximate. ETHICS: the region's later history under Kaabu is marked by extensive participation in the Atlantic slave trade — that falls under the existing post-1500 entity. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1300, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The existing DB entity 'Jolof' covers 1350-1549 (the unified imperial phase). This NEW entity covers the EARLY pre-imperial Wolof/Serer political formations c.1200-1350, when the Ndiadiane Ndiaye foundation myth describes a loose confederation of chiefdoms (Walo, Kayor, Baol, Dyolof, Sine, Saloum) before their consolidation. Sources are almost entirely Wolof oral tradition as recorded by 19th c. French observers — confidence is deliberately low. I include this because the existing DB treats Jolof as only emerging 1350, missing two centuries of precursor political formation. The alternative (skipping it) would collapse important gradual state-formation processes. ETHICS: distinguish from Sine (Siin) and Saalum which ARE in the DB as separate entities. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1279, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Rustamid imamate was the first Ibadi (Kharijite) state in the Maghreb, centered at Tahert (modern Tiaret, Algeria). Founded by Abd al-Rahman ibn Rustam, a Persian Ibadi missionary, and supported by Berber Ibadi tribes (especially the Nafusa and Lwata) who rejected both Umayyad and Abbasid Sunni orthodoxy. The state was destroyed by the Fatimids in 909 as part of the Isma'ili Shi'a conquest of the Maghreb. Ibadism still survives today in the Mzab (Algeria), Jerba (Tunisia), Nafusa (Libya), and Oman — a direct continuity from the Rustamids. Western scholarship long marginalized Ibadism as 'Kharijite sectarianism'; current historiography treats it as a major early Islamic movement with distinct theology. ETHICS: Rustamid society was notably egalitarian by medieval standards (imam elected, not hereditary in theory), but participated in trans-Saharan slave trading. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (800, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Rustamid Imamate
name_variants.source: Savage, E. A Gateway to Hell (1997)

name_variants.name: Imamat Rustamide
name_variants.source: Lewicki, T. in Revue des etudes islamiques (1962)

territory_changes.description: After the Ibadi Berber revolt was crushed by the Abbasids (771-772), the survivors gathered at Tahert and elected Abd al-Rahman ibn Rustam imam. This founded the first durable Ibadi state in North Africa.

Abu Abdullah al-Shi'i, the Fatimid da'i, conquered Tahert. The last Rustamid imam Yaqzan ibn Muhammad was killed. Ibadi communities survived by fleeing south to Sedrata, then to the Mzab oases, where they persist today.

sources.citation: Savage, Elizabeth. A Gateway to Hell, A Gateway to Paradise: The North African Response to the Arab Conquest. Princeton: Darwin Press, 1997
Lewicki, Tadeusz. 'The Ibadites in Arabia and Africa.' Journal of World History 13 (1971): 51-130

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Banu Midrar (Midrarid) emirate ruled Sijilmasa, the northern terminus of the western trans-Saharan gold trade, for roughly two centuries. Sijilmasa's prosperity derived from the gold-for-salt trade with Wagadou (Ghana) — the Midrarids were gatekeepers of one of the most important commercial arteries of the medieval world. The dynasty adhered to Sufri Kharijism, making them theological rivals of Sunni Umayyads and Abbasids alike. Fell to Fatimid then Umayyad control, then definitively to the Maghrawa Berber coalition in 976. Archaeological work by the Moroccan-American project at Sijilmasa (1988-1998) is the primary modern source. The site remained important into Almoravid times but never recovered its Midrarid-era autonomy. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (900, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Midrarid Dynasty
name_variants.source: Messier, R.A. and Miller, J.A. The Last Civilized Place (2015)

territory_changes.description: Abu al-Qasim Samgu ibn Wasul founded Sijilmasa as a Sufri Kharijite refuge polity, attracting dissidents from Abbasid authority. The town grew rapidly into the primary North African terminus of the Wangara gold trade.
The Maghrawa Berbers (Zenata confederation) captured Sijilmasa, ending Midrarid rule. The town continued under various overlords (Maghrawa, Almoravid, Merinid) but never again ruled itself.

sources.citation: Messier, Ronald A., and James A. Miller. The Last Civilized Place: Sijilmasa and Its Saharan Destiny. Austin: University of Texas Press, 2015
Lightfoot, Dale R., and James A. Miller. 'Sijilmassa: The Rise and Fall of a Walled Oasis in Medieval Morocco.' Annals of the Association of American Geographers 86, no. 1 (1996): 78-101

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Barghawata confederation is one of the most striking cases in Islamic history: a Berber state on the Atlantic coast of Morocco (Tamesna) that developed its OWN 'Quran' in Berber language, revealed to the prophet Salih ibn Tarif (d.~792), with its own prayer rituals, fast, and dietary laws. Orthodox Sunnis and Shi'a alike regarded the Barghawata as apostates and heretics. This parallel religious tradition lasted three centuries until finally crushed by the Almoravids (1058, with final extinction by Almohads 1148). ETHICS: virtually all surviving sources are from the Barghawata's enemies (Ibn Hawqal, al-Bakri, Ibn Khaldun), so the tradition is filtered through hostile lenses. Their 'heretical' theology is only preserved in polemic. This is a paradigmatic case of a religious movement suppressed into near-invisibility by victorious orthodoxy — the Barghawata deserve documentation precisely because conventional narratives tend to erase them. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (900, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.context: French spelling common in Maghrebi colonial-era scholarship

territory_changes.description: Salih ibn Tarif proclaimed himself prophet and founder of a new religion in the Sufri Kharijite tribal milieu of the Masmuda and Zenata Berbers of Tamesna. A distinctive Berber-language scripture and ritual developed over three generations.

Almoravid forces under Yusuf ibn Tashfin decisively defeated the Barghawata. Their king Abu Hafs was killed. Almoravid jihad rhetoric explicitly targeted Barghawata 'heresy'. Remnant communities were extinguished by Almohads by 1148.

sources.citation: Norris, H.T. The Berbers in Arabic Literature. London: Longman, 1982

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Hammadid emirate (central Maghreb, modern Algeria) was a Sanhaja Berber dynasty founded by Hammad ibn Buluggin, nephew of Zirid emir Badis. Initially a Zirid vassal in the western provinces, Hammad declared independence in 1014 and built the mountain capital Qal'at Bani Hammad (now a UNESCO site). Displaced to Bejaia (Bougie) c.1090 under pressure from the Banu Hilal. Surrendered to the Almohads in 1152. ETHICS: the Hammadids are frequently collapsed into Zirid histories because they were a cadet branch. Separating them documents the reality of medieval Maghreb as a mosaic of Berber dynasties rather than a succession of monolithic empires. The Qal'at site was extensively looted and destroyed after the Almohad conquest; archaeological work reveals a sophisticated urban civilization with elaborate hydraulic systems. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1100, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Hammadid Dynasty
name_variants.source: Idris, H.R. La Berberie orientale sous les Zirides (1962)

territory_changes.description: Hammad ibn Buluggin declared independence from his nephew the Zirid emir al-Mu'izz ibn Badis, founding the Hammadid dynasty at Qal'at Bani Hammad.

Under pressure from the Banu Hilal Arab migration disrupting the interior, the Hammadid capital shifted from the mountain fortress Qal'a to the coastal port of Bejaia, becoming a Mediterranean mercantile power.
Almohad caliph Abd al-Mu'min conquered Bejaia. The last Hammadid emir Yahya ibn Abd al-Aziz surrendered and was granted a pension in the Almohad retinue.

Golvin, Lucien. Recherches archeologiques a la Qal'a des Banu Hammad. Paris: Maisonneuve, 1965

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Mapungubwe is widely regarded as the first kingdom in southern Africa and the direct predecessor of Great Zimbabwe. The site (UNESCO World Heritage since 2003) demonstrates sophisticated social stratification: the sacred king and elite lived atop Mapungubwe Hill while commoners dwelt below — one of the earliest clear cases of class society in sub-Saharan Africa. Famous for the gold rhinoceros and other elite burial goods. ETHICS — APARTHEID CONTEXT: the site was excavated in 1933 under University of Pretoria sponsorship; the finds were then suppressed for decades because they contradicted apartheid ideology that claimed black Africans could not have built sophisticated civilizations in southern Africa. Mapungubwe material was kept in locked storage and access was restricted through the 1970s. Post-1994 South African scholarship and the UNESCO listing reversed this erasure. The predecessor sites K2 (Bambandyanalo, 1000-1075) and Schroda (900-1000) trace the state-formation trajectory. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1200, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Mapungubwe
name_variants.source: Huffman, T.N. Mapungubwe (2005)

name_variants.name: Mapungubwe
name_variants.context: Venda name meaning 'place of jackals' or 'hill of wisdom' (both etymologies proposed)

territory_changes.description: The capital shifted from K2 (Bambandyanalo) to Mapungubwe Hill, accompanied by formalization of sacred kingship, spatial segregation of elites, and peak Indian Ocean trade connections via Sofala/Chibuene (gold and ivory exports exchanged for glass beads, Chinese ceramics).

Mapungubwe was abandoned, likely due to climate change (the Medieval Warm Period ended and rainfall declined in the Shashe-Limpopo basin). Political and trade gravity shifted north to Great Zimbabwe, which rose to prominence immediately afterward.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: K2 (also called Bambandyanalo) is the immediate predecessor of Mapungubwe, occupied c.1000-1075 CE. Together with Schroda (900-1000), it represents the Leopard's Kopje sequence through which southern Africa's first state-level society developed. Separating K2 from Mapungubwe documents that state-formation was a gradual indigenous process, not a sudden import — a historiographically important point given colonial-era claims about 'Arab', 'Phoenician', or 'Hamitic' origins for southern African civilization. K2 lacked the spatial segregation of Mapungubwe; it is the transitional phase during which the Shashe-Limpopo polity grew large enough to produce class stratification. Confidence moderate because boundaries are archaeological reconstructions. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1000, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Butua was the southwestern Shona successor state to Great Zimbabwe, centered on Khami (UNESCO site since 1986, near modern Bulawayo). The ruling Torwa dynasty built stone enclosures smaller but more elaborately decorated than Great Zimbabwe (checker, cord, and herringbone wall patterns). Portuguese sources from the 16th century describe Butua as a wealthy gold-producing kingdom. Overthrown c.1683 when the Rozvi under Changamire Dombo conquered Khami; the existing DB entity 'Torwa' (Batch 29) and 'Rozwi'/'Rozvi' cover the successor state. Butua is the kingdom itself, pre-Rozvi conquest. ETHICS: Portuguese accounts of 'Monomotapa' often conflate Mutapa and Butua — they were distinct successor states and conflating them has distorted historiography.

territory_changes.description: Following the decline of Great Zimbabwe, the Torwa dynasty established the Butua kingdom at Khami, inheriting the Zimbabwe tradition of dry-stone elite architecture and Indian Ocean gold trade.
A succession dispute drew in Portuguese mercenaries under Sisnando Dias Bayao on one side. This is the high-water mark of Portuguese military intervention in the Zimbabwe plateau.
Changamire Dombo's Rozvi army defeated the Torwa, sacked Khami, and established the Rozvi Empire as the new southwestern Shona hegemon — covered in existing DB entity 'Rozwi'.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Thulamela (in modern Kruger National Park, South Africa) was a fortified stone-walled capital in the Zimbabwe cultural tradition, occupied by Venda and Lembe ancestors c.1240-1700. Excavations in the 1990s by the South African National Parks and University of Pretoria recovered gold beads, Chinese porcelain, and two royal burials (a king c.1450 and a queen c.1550) with grave goods demonstrating long-distance Indian Ocean trade connections. Thulamela was occupied concurrently with Great Zimbabwe, Khami (Butua), and Mutapa — it is the southernmost extension of the Zimbabwe stone-enclosure tradition and demonstrates that these polities extended across the Limpopo. ETHICS: the community descendants (Vha-Lembethu and Vha-Thavhatsindi Venda) were consulted in the 1990s re-excavation and reburial — a rare example of respectful post-apartheid archaeology contrasting with colonial-era grave-robbing practices. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1492, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The existing DB entity 'Reth e Collo' (Shilluk kingdom) covers the period from Nyikango's traditional founding c.1490 forward. This entity covers the uncertain PRE-FORMATION period c.1450-1490 when Luo migration into the Upper Nile was under way but Nyikango's paramount chieftainship had not yet been established. Sources are almost entirely Shilluk oral tradition. Confidence is very low by design. I include it only because existing DB begins abruptly at 1490 with no precursor. If considered too speculative during ingest, this entry can be skipped — flagged in README. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1492, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The existing DB 'Bunyoro-Kitara' starts c.1500 with the Babito (Luo) dynasty. This NEW entity covers the PRIOR Chwezi/Cwezi-associated empire of Kitara c.1300-1500, centered on the earthwork complexes of Bigo bya Mugenyi, Ntusi, and Mubende Hill. Chwezi traditions are semi-mythological — Ndahura, Wamala, and Bucwezi are culture-heroes in Nyoro/Tooro/Ankole/Hema oral tradition, worshipped in Cwezi spirit-possession cults still practiced today. Archaeology (Posnansky, Sutton, Robertshaw) confirms large earthworks and iron-working centers of this era but the political structure is debated: some scholars argue the Chwezi were historical rulers; others treat them as legendary ancestors to which real earthworks were retroactively attributed. I deliberately set confidence low to reflect this. ETHICS: colonial-era 'Hamitic hypothesis' racially attributed Cwezi achievements to supposed Caucasoid immigrants — modern scholarship rejects this firmly as racist pseudo-science. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1400, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Engaruka (northern Tanzania, Rift Valley floor) is the single largest pre-colonial stone-built agricultural complex in East Africa — approximately 20 km² of terraced fields, stone-built dwellings, and elaborate irrigation channels. Occupied c.1400-1700 by an agricultural society that vanished, probably when the spring-fed irrigation failed due to tectonic and hydrological changes. Excavated by Louis Leakey, Hamo Sassoon, and later John Sutton. ETHICS: Engaruka contradicts the colonial narrative that East Africans practiced only 'shifting cultivation' — it demonstrates complex, sedentary, water-managed agriculture. The identity of the builders is uncertain; Iraqw, Sonjo, and Maasai traditions all refer to the ruins. I use 'iak' (Iraqw) as the most probable linguistic affiliation per Sutton's analysis, but confidence is moderate. Not a kingdom — more a distributed polity whose specific governance is archaeologically unclear, so entity_type is set to 'settlement-complex'.

name_variants.name: Engaruka
name_variants.context: standard rendering of the site name
name_variants.source: Sutton, J.E.G. A Thousand Years of East Africa (1990)

territory_changes.description: Engaruka was abandoned, probably because the tectonically fed springs that powered the irrigation system dried up. No violent destruction layer is visible. The population dispersed, likely into surrounding Sonjo and Iraqw communities.

sources.citation: Sutton, John E.G. A Thousand Years of East Africa. Nairobi: British Institute in Eastern Africa, 1990
Stump, Daryl. 'The Development and Expansion of the Field and Irrigation System at Engaruka, Tanzania.' Azania 41 (2006): 69-94

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Bigo bya Mugenyi ('fortress of the strangers') is the largest pre-colonial earthwork complex in East Africa — roughly 10 km of interconnected ditches enclosing an area near the Katonga River in Uganda. Radiocarbon dated to c.1300-1500 CE. Oral tradition attributes the earthworks to the Cwezi, making Bigo a ceremonial capital of the Kitara empire. Scholarly debate continues about whether Bigo was a royal capital, a cattle kraal, or a ritual center — or all three. I classify it here as a distinct archaeological entity rather than folding it into 'Kitara Empire' because its physical extent and unique ditch system merit separate geographic registration. ETHICS: Bigo and associated sites (Ntusi, Kibengo, Kagogo, Mubende Hill) form a system — the Kitara entry references them but a separate Bigo entity allows precise localization. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1400, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

territory_changes.description: Bigo was abandoned around 1500, coinciding with the transition from Cwezi to Babito rule in the interlacustrine region. The earthwork system was never repaired or reused.

sources.citation: Posnansky, Merrick. 'Bigo bya Mugenyi.' Uganda Journal 33, no. 2 (1969): 125-150

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Nok culture of central Nigeria is the earliest known producer of large-scale terracotta sculpture in sub-Saharan Africa and one of the earliest iron-smelting societies in West Africa (evidence from c.500 BCE, possibly earlier). Discovered by tin miners in 1928 and systematically excavated only from 2005 onward by the Goethe University Frankfurt / National Commission for Museums and Monuments Nigeria joint project. The political structure of Nok society is unknown — it may have been acephalous villages rather than a state. I use entity_type='culture' to reflect this. ETHICS — SEVERE LOOTING: from the 1990s, illegal excavation devastated Nok sites. Thousands of terracotta heads were looted and sold on the European art market (Brussels was a major hub). Nigerian authorities have sought restitution. The Louvre's 2000 acquisition of two Nok pieces provoked a major diplomatic dispute. Many authentic pieces in Western collections were looted after 1970 (Unesco Convention). Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-500, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Nok culture
name_variants.source: Rupp, N. in Journal of African Archaeology (2005)

territory_changes.description: The Nok culture declined around 500 CE for reasons that remain unclear. Possible factors include soil exhaustion, climate change, or population dispersal. There is no evidence of violent destruction.

sources.citation: Rupp, Nicole. 'New Studies on the Nok Culture of Central Nigeria.' Journal of African Archaeology 3, no. 2 (2005): 283-290
Breunig, Peter. Nok: African Sculpture in Archaeological Context. Frankfurt: Africa Magna Verlag, 2014

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Djenne-Djeno ('ancient Djenne') in the Inland Niger Delta of Mali is one of the earliest cities in sub-Saharan Africa, continuously occupied c.250 BCE to 1400 CE. Excavated by Roderick and Susan McIntosh (1977, 1981) who demonstrated sophisticated urbanism predating Arab trans-Saharan trade contact by nearly a millennium — a decisive refutation of the colonial-era 'Hamitic hypothesis' that claimed African civilizations arose only through North African/Middle Eastern stimulus. Djenne-Djeno grew to ~33 ha with ~27 associated satellite sites at its peak (c.800-1250). ETHICS — LOOTING: from the 1980s extensive illegal excavation devastated Inland Delta terracotta sites; Djenne-Djeno terracottas became a hot item in the European antiquities market. Mali and Unesco imposed restrictions in 1993. Djenne-Djeno is distinct from the still-inhabited modern Djenne (famous for the Great Mosque) which is 3 km away and grew after Djenne-Djeno's abandonment.

name_variants.name: Jenne-jeno
name_variants.source: McIntosh, R.J. The Peoples of the Middle Niger (1998)

territory_changes.description: Djenne-Djeno reached its maximum extent (~33 ha) and engaged in long-distance iron, copper, and gold trade. Population estimates run 10,000-20,000 inhabitants, making it one of West Africa's largest urban centers of the period.

Djenne-Djeno was abandoned around 1400 as occupation shifted to the nearby site of modern Djenne. Causes include the Islamization of the region (under Mali influence) and possibly shifting river courses. The transition appears peaceful.

sources.citation: McIntosh, Roderick J., and Susan Keech McIntosh. Prehistoric Investigations in the Region of Jenne, Mali. Oxford: BAR International Series 89, 1980
McIntosh, Roderick J. The Peoples of the Middle Niger: The Island of Gold. Oxford: Blackwell, 1998
McIntosh, Susan Keech (ed.). Excavations at Jenne-Jeno, Hambarketolo, and Kaniana. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1995

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The existing DB entity 'Songhai' covers the Songhai Empire (1464-1591) under the Sonni and Askia dynasties. This entity covers the PRE-IMPERIAL kingdom of Gao (Kawkaw/Gao-Saney) c.600-1275 CE, which was the earlier Songhai-speaking polity at the Niger bend before being absorbed by Mali c.1275 and re-emerging as the imperial Songhai in 1464. The Gao-Saney cemetery excavated by Insoll and Cisse produced Muslim tombstones from the 11th c. with the earliest Arabic inscriptions yet found in sub-Saharan Africa. The Za dynasty is recorded in Tarikh al-Sudan and Tarikh al-Fattash — the first Muslim Za ruler Kossoi reportedly converted c.1010. ETHICS: separating this from imperial Songhai is historiographically important because it documents the 600+ year pre-imperial Songhai political tradition that is usually flattened into the imperial narrative. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (900, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Koumbi Saleh (southeastern Mauritania) is the generally accepted archaeological identification of the capital of Wagadou/Ghana described by al-Bakri in 1068. The existing DB entity 'Wagadou' uses Koumbi Saleh as its capital, so why a separate entity? Because Koumbi Saleh as an URBAN SITE continued to be occupied after Wagadou's political end (1200) and passed through Sosso and early Mali rule. The city is thus temporally longer than the Wagadou state. Additionally, al-Bakri's description of a 'twin-city' with a separate Muslim merchant town suggests a distinctive urban form that deserves its own registration. ETHICS: the identification of Koumbi Saleh with al-Bakri's Ghana is contested; some scholars (Masonen, Levtzion) note that al-Bakri never visited and his account is based on merchant reports. Confidence is moderate because of this and because the medieval name of the site itself is uncertain (Koumbi may be a modern Soninke name). Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1000, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Wadan (also Ouadane), in modern Mauritania, was a major trans-Saharan caravan town founded traditionally 1141 by Berber and Arab Idaw al-Hajj clan. Together with Chinguetti, Tichit, and Oualata, Wadan forms the UNESCO-listed 'Ancient Ksour' of Mauritania. Wadan was the first West African terminus where the Portuguese attempted to establish direct trade (1487, factory abandoned 1489) — a significant early episode of Atlantic-Saharan commercial integration. The town was an Islamic learning center whose manuscript library still preserves important medieval texts. Decline from late 16th c. as trans-Saharan trade shifted eastward. ETHICS: the Atlantic slave trade and colonial French rule disrupted the Sahelian caravan economy; Wadan's decline cannot be separated from these external factors.

name_variants.name: Ouadane
name_variants.source: Cleaveland, T. Becoming Walata (2002)

territory_changes.description: Portuguese merchants under Diogo d'Azambuja established a factory at Wadan attempting to capture the gold-salt trade at its source. The factory was abandoned by 1489 due to local hostility and logistical difficulties — one of the earliest failures of direct European commercial penetration into West Africa's interior.
Start of gradual decline as trans-Saharan trade shifted eastward to Timbuktu-Gao axis and southward to Atlantic coastal routes.

sources.citation: Cleaveland, Timothy. Becoming Walata: A History of Saharan Social Formation and Transformation. Portsmouth, NH: Heinemann, 2002
Marty, Paul. Etudes sur l'Islam et les tribus du Soudan. Vol. 3: Les tribus maures du Sahel et du Hodh. Paris: Ernest Leroux, 1921

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Oualata (Walata, Biru) was one of the four caravan cities of medieval Mauritania (with Chinguetti, Tichit, Wadan). Visited and described in detail by Ibn Battuta in 1352 as his first stop after crossing the Sahara — he described the matrilineal Massufa Berber society with a critical Islamic gaze, noting women's social freedom that scandalized him. Oualata was the successor commercial center to Awdaghust after Almoravid-era decline of the latter. Passed through Mali and Songhai tributary status. The Oualata house-decoration tradition (geometric painted facades) survives today and is a significant element of Mauritanian intangible heritage. ETHICS: Ibn Battuta's account is a rare first-hand medieval source on sub-Saharan society; his misogynist criticism of Massufa gender norms is itself important evidence of plural Islamic cultural practice. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1300, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Tichitt Tradition (roughly 2000-300 BCE) is one of the earliest complex societies in West Africa, pre-dating Djenne-Djeno by a millennium. Drystone-walled villages along the Tichitt-Oualata escarpment in southeastern Mauritania demonstrate sedentary cereal agriculture (Pennisetum glaucum / pearl millet — one of the earliest domesticated African cereals here), social hierarchy, and long-distance exchange. Excavations by Patrick Munson and Augustin Holl established a four-phase sequence culminating in the Dakhlet el Atrouss phase (c.1100-400 BCE) with possibly proto-urban settlements of ~10,000 population. ETHICS: the Tichitt Tradition is direct ancestor to the Soninke and Wagadou — an indigenous West African trajectory of state formation with no external stimulus. It dismantles the colonial-era claim that sub-Saharan urbanism required Berber or Arab influence. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-1000, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Awdaghust (identified archaeologically with Tegdaoust, Mauritania) was the southern terminus of the Sijilmasa-Awdaghust trans-Saharan route and the commercial rival-then-tributary of Wagadou/Ghana. Described by al-Bakri (1068) as a large Berber city inhabited by Zanata Berbers, with sophisticated water management and close commercial ties to Wagadou. Conquered by Wagadou before 990; sacked by the Almoravids in 1054/1055. Excavated by the French archaeological mission (Polet, Robert, Devisse) 1960-1976. ETHICS: the 1054 Almoravid sack of Awdaghust is significant evidence against the popular narrative that Almoravids 'conquered Ghana' in 1076 — Almoravid pressure on Wagadou went back at least 20 years and via Awdaghust. Current historiography (Masonen, Conrad & Fisher) questions whether a single 1076 conquest even happened. ethical_notes: ETHICS: The existing DB entity 'Mengist Ityop'p'ya' (Ethiopian Empire, batch 29) covers the full Solomonic span to 1974. This NEW entity specifically carves out the EARLY Solomonic period 1270-1500 — from Yekuno Amlak's foundation through the mobile court (katama) era, before the establishment of Gondar. This 230-year period is historiographically distinct: no fixed capital (roving royal camps), evangelical expansion into southern pagan and Muslim regions, the reigns of Amda Seyon I (r.1314-1344) and Zara Yaqob (r.1434-1468) that defined Ethiopian imperial ideology, and the initial encounter with Portuguese/European powers (1490s onward). ETHICS: Yekuno Amlak's 'restoration' of the Solomonic line over the Zagwe was ideological — the claim to Solomonic descent via the Queen of Sheba legend (Kebra Nagast) was codified to legitimize the dynasty. Amda Seyon's conquests southward produced substantial forced Christianization of Muslim and traditional-religion peoples. IMPORTANT: since 'Mengist Ityop'p'ya' already covers this span, this may be treated as duplicate during ingest; I flag it as optional and recommend skipping unless the DB architecture supports phase-specific sub-entities. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1400, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

End of the early Solomonic period approximately coincides with the Portuguese embassy of 1520 and the start of the Adal-Ethiopian jihad (1527-1543, already covered in DB 'Sultanat Adal' entry) which devastated the kingdom.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Zeila (Saylac, in modern northwestern Somalia) was a major Islamic port-city and the principal gateway for trans-Red Sea trade between the Horn of Africa and the Arabian Peninsula for over 700 years. Described by al-Yaqubi (c.872), al-Masudi (c.950), and Ibn Battuta (1331). Served as the primary port of the Adal Sultanate (already in DB) during its heyday. Zeila was a center of Islamic learning — the Qadiriyya Sufi order spread from here into the Ethiopian highlands. Sacked by the Portuguese in 1517 (Lopo Soares de Albergaria) and again in 1528 (Antonio da Saldanha). ETHICS: treating Zeila as a separate city-state distinct from the Adal Sultanate is historiographically justified because the city had periods of autonomy before, during, and after Adal and was inhabited continuously for over a millennium. Ibn Battuta called it the dirtiest city he had ever seen (among many negative judgments about African cities, revealing his own biases).

territory_changes.description: Ibn Battuta visited Zeila, describing it as a Muslim city inhabited by Somali Berberah people engaged in Red Sea trade. His harsh judgments about the city's sanitation and inhabitants are a notable example of medieval intra-Muslim cultural prejudice.
Portuguese fleet under Lopo Soares de Albergaria sacked and burned Zeila as part of their campaign to disrupt Muslim Red Sea trade networks. A second sack followed in 1528.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Berbera was a major seasonal Red Sea trading port inhabited by Somalis, described by Arab geographers (al-Idrisi, Ibn Sa'id) from the 12th c. onward. Periodically autonomous, periodically tributary to Adal, Ajuuraan, and later Ottoman and Omani powers. The seasonal trading fair was a regional institution — ships from Mocha, Aden, and the Persian Gulf converged during the northeast monsoon to trade for Somali livestock, aromatics (myrrh, frankincense), and slaves. The surrounding Land of Punt / Bilad al-Barbar had been trading across the Red Sea since Pharaonic times (Hatshepsut's expedition c.1479 BCE). ETHICS: the pre-1500 period is deliberately bounded to satisfy the user request; Berbera's later history is already partly in DB via 'Isaaq'/Somali entities (checking: the DB does have 'Ajuuraan', 'Warsangeli' for Horn but not Berbera specifically). ethical_notes: ETHICS: Ntusi (western Uganda) is the largest pre-Bigo earthwork/earth-mound site in the Great Lakes interlacustrine region, with massive middens (up to 10m high) reflecting long-term cattle-keeping and iron-working occupation c.1000-1400. Predecessor to the Cwezi-era Bigo complex. Associated with the proto-state formation of the region (ancestral Kitara). Recent isotopic analysis (Robertshaw) shows massive cattle concentration, suggesting Ntusi was a political-ritual center accumulating wealth-on-the-hoof. ETHICS: Ntusi's moat-and-mound form became the template for the later Bigo earthwork system. Excluded from the Bunyoro-pre-Babito entity because its earlier dating and distinct archaeological character justify a separate record. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1200, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Essouk (identified with medieval Tadmakka, 'resembling Mecca' in Berber, in northern Mali) was a Berber city-state and major Saharan entrepot between the 9th and 14th centuries. Al-Bakri (1068) described Tadmakka as richer than any city in the Western Sudan except Wagadou. Excavated by Sam Nixon 2005-2010; finds include gold-processing evidence (imitation dinars molds), early Arabic and Tifinagh inscriptions, and extensive Muslim tombstones. ETHICS: Tadmakka was the seat of the Berber Banu Tanamak dynasty, a Sufri Kharijite lineage like the Midrarids. The Banu Tanamak claimed descent from early companions of the Prophet — a classic claim of noble Muslim ancestry in a frontier context. Distinct from the Takedda entity above: Tadmakka is NW of Takedda and considerably larger and better-documented.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Garoumele (modern eastern Niger, near the Komadugu Yobe river) is identified by several scholars (Lange, Connah) as one of the possible sites of Njimi, the medieval Kanem capital, or as a major Kanem provincial center. The site has extensive fired-brick architectural remains — unusual for the Sahel, suggesting elite occupation. It was a significant Kanem site in the 13th-15th centuries before being abandoned after the Bulala disruption. Confidence is moderate because the Njimi identification is disputed (other candidates include N'jimi further north). Listing Garoumele as a distinct settlement documents the Kanem-era built environment independently of the state-level Kanem entry. ethical_notes: ETHICS: Bono-Manso (also 'Bono Manso', in central Ghana) was the earliest known Akan state, founded c.1270 by the Bono people under the legendary king Asaman. The Akan gold trade — later the economic foundation of Asante — was initially directed northward from Bono-Manso to Dyula merchants at Bighu and then across the Sahara. The site was excavated by Effah-Gyamfi 1972-1973, revealing sophisticated urban architecture and extensive goldworking debris. The existing DB has 'Asante' (batch 03, starting 1670) but no Bono-Manso, creating a major gap in pre-1500 Akan history. ETHICS: Asante's imperial history is well-documented; the earlier Akan polity of Bono-Manso is marginalized in both colonial and post-colonial narratives that privilege Asante founding myths. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1400, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Bighu (Bitu, in northern Ghana/southern Burkina Faso area) was a major Dyula (Mande Muslim merchant) entrepot linking the Akan goldfields to the trans-Saharan trade via Djenne. Attested in Arabic geographical sources c.1500 (Leo Africanus describes 'Bitu' as wealthy from gold). Archaeological identification is uncertain; the town was probably at or near modern Bego in Brong-Ahafo. The Dyula of Bighu, according to tradition, were forebears of the later Watara of Kong. ETHICS: Bighu is historiographically marginalized as a purely commercial node without state-level political authority. But it was economically one of the most important pre-1500 towns in West Africa, transmitting Akan gold northward to fuel the trans-Saharan trade. Low confidence due to scant archaeology. ethical_notes: ETHICS: Vumba Kuu ('Great Vumba', in the modern Kenya-Tanzania border area near Vanga) was a Swahili city-state that ruled the southern Kenya coast before falling under Mombasa and Omani overlordship. Ruled by the Shirazi dynasty of mythological Persian origin (a common Swahili elite founder narrative). Archaeologically attested by ruins with coral-rag architecture, mosques, and tombs. Included because the existing DB covers major Swahili city-states (Kilwa, Mombasa, Malindi, Pate, Lamu, Pemba, Zanzibar, Sofala, Angoche) but not the smaller-yet-historically-significant Vumba/Tumbatu/Mafia cluster. ETHICS: the 'Shirazi' foundation narrative was long read as historical evidence of Persian colonization; modern historiography (Horton, Middleton, Pouwels) reads it as indigenous African Islamic self-fashioning borrowing Persian prestige. ethical_notes: ETHICS: Manda (in the Lamu archipelago, Kenya) was one of the EARLIEST documented Swahili urban settlements, founded c.800 and flourishing through the 13th century before declining in favor of nearby Lamu. Excavated by Neville Chittick 1965-1978 and Mark Horton subsequently. Manda produced among the earliest dated mosques and imported ceramics on the Swahili coast, demonstrating Islamic and Indian Ocean commercial integration already in the 9th century — pre-dating the common narrative that Swahili urbanism arose only in the 11th-12th c. This timing is important historiographically: it shows the Swahili coast was commercially connected to the Abbasid Caliphate at the peak of the Baghdad era. ethical_notes: ETHICS: Shanga (Pate Island, Kenya) is one of the most thoroughly excavated early Swahili sites (Horton 1980-1988) and has produced crucial evidence for the ORIGINS of Swahili civilization. The site shows continuous occupation from c.750, with the earliest known mosque (late 8th c., pre-dating any other on the coast) built of timber, then rebuilt in stone. Burials include Muslim-style (east-west, flexed) and traditional-style (grave goods), suggesting a hybrid phase of conversion. ETHICS — historiographically critical: Shanga's evidence demolishes the old 'Arab colonists' theory of Swahili origins. The site shows local iron-age African populations gradually adopting Islam and Indian Ocean trade — an indigenous African Islamic civilization, not an Arab colonial outpost. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1100, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Mafia island (off the Tanzanian coast) hosted a sultanate at Kisimani Mafia from approximately the 11th to 16th centuries. Archaeologically attested by coral-rag ruins, imported Chinese porcelain (Song and Ming), and a congregational mosque. Politically at times autonomous, at times tributary to Kilwa (already in DB). Kisimani Mafia was eclipsed by Kua (on another part of the island) and then by the mainland Portuguese-Omani power struggles of the 16th-17th c. Confidence moderate because the sultanate's precise political structure is archaeologically inferred rather than textually documented. ETHICS: Mafia's inclusion completes the Swahili-coast polity map; the DB's existing Swahili-coast entities concentrate on Kenya and far-northern Tanzania, neglecting the Mafia/Kilwa hinterland cluster.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Tumbatu is a small island off the northwestern coast of Unguja (Zanzibar). The Tumbatu sultanate flourished c.1100-1500, with a Shirazi-tradition dynasty and a large coral-rag urban complex (the Tumbatu ruins extend over ~20 ha). According to the Kilwa Chronicle, Tumbatu briefly served as the place of exile of Kilwa sultans — indicating it was a significant political node in the Zanzibar channel trading system. Distinct from the existing DB 'Zanzibar Sultanate' entity which starts 1856 (Omani phase). ETHICS: documenting pre-Omani, pre-Portuguese Zanzibar archipelago politics is essential because the Omani narrative has flattened the deep African-Islamic history of these islands.

name_variants.name: Tumbatu
name_variants.source: Horton, M. in Azania (1984)

territory_changes.description: Tumbatu's political autonomy eroded by the early 16th c. as Portuguese then Omani powers reorganized the archipelago. The town was progressively abandoned but the island remained inhabited.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Chwaka (on Pemba, Tanzania) is one of several medieval stone-town sites on Pemba — part of a dense Swahili-coast urban network that has only recently been systematically investigated (LaViolette, Fleisher 2000s). Pemba was ruled by various Shirazi-tradition dynasties before Portuguese intervention. Adding Chwaka addresses a gap: the DB has 'Pemba' but as a single island-level entity; Chwaka was one of several autonomous Pemba stone-towns. ETHICS: low-confidence decision — but adding it documents the archaeological reality that Pemba was politically fragmented, not a single kingdom, during the medieval period. ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Garamantes were a Berber kingdom of the Fezzan (modern southwestern Libya) whose capital Garama (modern Germa) controlled the central Saharan trade routes for over a millennium. Their sophisticated foggara (qanat-type) irrigation system supported urban agriculture in the desert. Classical Greek and Roman sources (Herodotus, Pliny) described them as 'raiding' and 'barbaric' — the Roman emperor Septimius Severus claimed victory over them c.203 CE. Modern archaeology (David Mattingly, 1997-2011 Fezzan Project) has revealed a sedentary, literate urban society whose slavery-supported economy enabled ambitious hydraulic engineering. ETHICS — slavery: the Garamantes captured Saharan populations to maintain the foggara system through labor-intensive maintenance, a precursor to the medieval trans-Saharan slave trade. This is essential context. Not to be confused with the existing DB 'Numidia' (further west, Algerian plateau).

name_variants.name: Garamantes
name_variants.context: Greek/Latin designation — the original Berber self-name is lost
name_variants.source: Herodotus, Histories IV.183

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Garama
name_variants.context: English academic designation focused on the capital
name_variants.source: Mattingly, D. The Archaeology of Fazzan (2003-2013)

territory_changes.description: Cornelius Balbus led a Roman expedition into the Fezzan, reaching Garama. This is the only Roman military campaign into the central Sahara, rewarded with a triumph — after which the Garamantes became Roman clients rather than being annexed.

The Garamantian state collapsed around the time of the Arab Muslim conquest of the Maghreb. The foggara system could not be maintained as the slave-labor economy and central political authority dissolved. Populations survived in diminished Fezzan oasis towns.

sources.citation: Mattingly, David J., ed. The Archaeology of Fazzan. 4 vols. London: Society for Libyan Studies, 2003-2013
Liverani, Mario, ed. Aghram Nadharif: The Barkat Oasis in the Fezzan (Southwest Libya). Rome: Arid Zone Archaeology Monographs, 2005

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Blemmyes (Greek Blemmyai, Egyptian Medjay) were a Beja-speaking pastoralist confederation of the Eastern Desert between the Nile and the Red Sea. They raided Roman Egypt persistently from the 3rd c. onward, forcing Emperor Diocletian to withdraw the Roman frontier to the First Cataract (297 CE). They maintained a cult center at the Temple of Isis at Philae until Justinian closed it (c.535-537), one of the last functioning pagan temples in the ancient world. Modern Beja peoples of eastern Sudan and Eritrea are their descendants. ETHICS: Roman and Byzantine sources are uniformly hostile ('barbarians', 'raiders'); the Blemmyan perspective is preserved only in a handful of Greek and Demotic documents from Philae. They were neither the 'headless monsters' of Pliny's imagination nor mere raiders — they ran a complex transhumant economy linking the Red Sea coast to the Nile valley. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-1, strategy=exact_name, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Blemmyai
name_variants.source: Strabo, Geography 17.1.53

name_variants.name: Medjay
name_variants.context: ancient Egyptian designation for Eastern Desert peoples, possibly same as Blemmyes
name_variants.source: Zibelius-Chen, K. in Studien zur Altagyptischen Kultur (1988)

name_variants.name: Beja (ancestral)
name_variants.source: Dahl, G. and Hjort-af-Ornas, A. in Nomadic Peoples (2006)

territory_changes.description: Diocletian withdrew the Roman frontier from Primis (Qasr Ibrim) to Syene (Aswan), ceding the Dodekaschoinos. The Blemmyes and the allied Nobatae were settled as foederati to guard the new frontier in exchange for subsidies. This is one of the clearest cases of a Saharan/African confederation successfully negotiating with a Mediterranean empire.

Justinian closed the Temple of Isis at Philae, ending the last openly pagan cult of the Blemmyes. Blemmyan raids on Egypt ceased by the 6th c. as Christian Nubian kingdoms (Makuria, Nobadia, Alodia) stabilized the frontier.

sources.citation: Updegraff, Robert T. 'The Blemmyes I: The Rise of the Blemmyes and the Roman Withdrawal from Nubia.' In Aufstieg und Niedergang der romischen Welt II.10.1, 44-97. Berlin: De Gruyter, 1988
Pierce, Richard H., trans. Fontes Historiae Nubiorum, Vol. 3. Bergen: University of Bergen, 1998

ethical_notes: ETHICS: NOTE — this entity is POST-1500 (1591-1833), so technically outside the user's pre-1500 scope. However it fills an important gap: the period between Songhai's destruction (1591, existing DB covers Songhai's end) and the Fulani-Tukulor conquests of the mid-19th century. The Arma (also Ruma) were the descendants of Judar Pasha's Moroccan conquest army (Andalusian renegades, Spanish converts, Moroccan Berbers) who established a pashalik at Timbuktu under nominal Saadian Moroccan authority until 1612, then became independent. They ruled the Niger bend oases for 240+ years. Including this because the user request explicitly mentioned 'expand if you find other' relevant gaps — the Arma pashalik is a major gap in DB coverage of West Africa. If strict pre-1500 adherence is required, skip. Otherwise, include.

name_variants.name: Pashalik of Timbuktu
name_variants.source: Hunwick, J. Timbuktu and the Songhay Empire (1999)

name_variants.name: Arma
name_variants.source: al-Sadi, Tarikh al-Sudan

territory_changes.description: Judar Pasha's Saadian Moroccan army destroyed the Songhai at the Battle of Tondibi and established a pashalik governing Timbuktu, Djenne, and Gao. The scholarly community of Timbuktu was decimated; Ahmad Baba was deported to Marrakesh.

The Arma pashalik became de facto independent of the Saadian court, though pashas were still appointed by the soldiery from their own ranks. A local creole culture emerged, with Arabic as administrative language and Songhai as vernacular.
The Fulani Massina Empire under Seku Amadu conquered Timbuktu, ending the Arma pashalik. Arma communities persisted but lost political power.

sources.citation: Hunwick, John O. Timbuktu and the Songhay Empire: Al-Sa'di's Ta'rikh al-Sudan down to 1613. Leiden: Brill, 1999
Kaba, Lansine. 'Archers, Musketeers, and Mosquitoes: The Moroccan Invasion of the Sudan and the Songhay Resistance (1591-1612).' Journal of African History 22, no. 4 (1981): 457-475

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The existing DB entity 'Takrur' (batch 28) exists and covers the kingdom — but with a specific temporal range that may not cover the EARLIEST Serer-Fulani-Tukulor phase (c.500-1040) before the Almoravid-era Islamic conversion. This new entity addresses that potential gap. If DB's 'Takrur' already starts at 500, SKIP this entry — it would be a duplicate. If DB's 'Takrur' starts later (e.g., 1040), include. The War Jabi conversion to Islam c.1040 (first documented Muslim sub-Saharan ruler) is a hinge event. Flag in README for ingest review.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: This entity covers the pre-imperial Yoruba polity cluster (other than Ile-Ife, which has its own entry above) — roughly the period of the earliest Oyo-Ile, Ijebu, Owo, and related Yoruba proto-states before the rise of the Oyo Empire (existing DB entity 'Oyo'). Sources are almost entirely oral tradition (the various Itan lineage histories). Confidence deliberately low. Oduduwa myth places the 'sixteen crowned kings' (owning 'beaded crowns' as sign of Ile-Ife ritual descent) as the ancestors of all Yoruba obaships — a retrospective projection of Ile-Ife paramountcy onto polities that may have been independent in origin. ethical_notes: ETHICS: Punt (Egyptian Pwnt) was the 'Land of the God' from which Egypt obtained incense, gold, ebony, ivory, myrrh, and exotic animals from at least the 25th c. BCE. Its geographical location is one of the most durable debates in Egyptology — candidates include the Eritrean-Sudanese coast, Somalia, and even Yemen. Current consensus (based on isotope analysis of Hatshepsut's baboons from Punt at Cairo Museum) favors the eastern African coast in modern Eritrea/Djibouti/northern Somalia. Queen Hatshepsut's famous expedition c.1479 BCE is depicted in reliefs at Deir el-Bahri showing Punt houses on stilts, a bearded king, and a queen with steatopygia — invaluable ethnographic evidence. ETHICS: low confidence because literally everything about Punt's political organization is uncertain — it may not have been a kingdom in the Egyptian sense but a loose coalition of coastal chiefdoms. I list it at 0.4. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (-2000, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Pwnt
name_variants.source: Hatshepsut, Deir el-Bahri reliefs (c.1479 BCE)

name_variants.name: Land of Punt
name_variants.source: Kitchen, K.A. in Orientalia Lovaniensia (1993)

name_variants.name: Ta Netjer
name_variants.context: Egyptian 'Land of the God', an honorific

territory_changes.description: Hatshepsut's Punt expedition returned with five shiploads of myrrh trees, gold, ebony, baboons, leopard skins, and 31 live myrrh trees (the first documented botanical transplantation in history). The Deir el-Bahri reliefs preserve this record in unprecedented detail.
Contact between Egypt and Punt declined after the 20th Dynasty (c.1000 BCE) as Egyptian central authority weakened. Punt disappears from Egyptian records.

Dominy, Nathaniel J., et al. 'Mummified Baboons Reveal the Far Reach of Early Egyptian Mariners.' eLife 9 (2020): e60860

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Daju were the first state-forming people of Darfur (western Sudan), preceding the Tunjur and then the Keira Sultanate covered by existing DB 'Saltanat Dar Fur'. Oral traditions (Macmichael, O'Fahey) place the Daju c.1200-1400. The capital Uri is an archaeological site with stone ruins. Very low confidence because nearly all sources are 19th-20th c. oral tradition retrojected. ETHICS: including this documents pre-Keira political life in Darfur, a historiographically marginalized region. If ingest finds evidence too thin, skip.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Tunjur were the second state-forming people of Darfur, ruling c.1400-1640 before being overthrown by the Keira (existing DB entity 'Saltanat Dar Fur').
